metric_value: Let me make clear that 64-bit computing has been around for quite some time now, and in fact the whole UNIX RISC area already is 64-bit capable. We see it being used particularly for handling large, scalable [data].

The majority of consumer market for 64-bit computing will arrive at the end of the decade. We expect mainstream desktops to need full 64-bit addressability of RAM by 2010 due to declining memory prices, more OS features, and web services.
notes: Kastner key prediction — substantially validated by Windows 7 (2009)

metric_value: Drivers: declining memory prices, more OS features (security, virtualization), and web-services-driven application growth requiring larger memory footprints

The 64-bit market is dominated by high-end proprietary systems. This will change dramatically over the remainder of the decade. Industry-standard 64-bit architectures will move down to the low-end and up into the mid-range and high-end.
notes: Aggregate analyst consensus quote curated by AMD

metric_value: In the next two to three years, one will witness a large set of enterprises going in for 64-bit systems as it offers superior performance in terms of scalability, power and reliability
Transition for pervasive 64-bit computing is going to take some time. The server side is well familiar with this technology; the trick is on a desktop, where this is going to take longer.
I believe that 64-bit technology will bring new and exciting opportunities to the mainstream market. Once you give millions [of users 64-bit, the ecosystem follows].
Peter Kastner attributed as Aberdeen Group analyst in AMD industry-visionary curation
